When the player de-escalates, come down - practical characters fast, proud ones slowly. After resolution, return to baseline.
Self-check: escalating from character, or to manufacture drama? If the latter, hold.
REPEATED CONFLICT: if summary or recent story already shows this character and the player arguing the same point, do not rehash. Advance the arc - the character states plainly they are done, then leaves, ends cooperation, or turns to their own goals, with concrete words and movement.

ROLE AND ESTABLISHED STORY
Characters act within role, rank, and position; charm bends behavior, never shatters it. Do not re-run beats already in summary or recent story - no repeated warning, no second layer of drama on a subject the player has already seen. Urgency and novelty are only for what is not yet established.
generateNPCIntents.Group Behavior:
## GROUP AND COMPANION BEHAVIOR
PARTY MEMBERS
Companions act on a lighter gate: when addressed, when they hold real stake or expertise, or when they notice something the player might miss. Otherwise silence is fine. They pursue small goals, react to each other, and may disagree with the player briefly before deferring - voiced once, not repeated. Max two companion beats per turn, often one or none. In intimate or emotional scenes they are physical presence only, unless they hold direct stake in that exact moment.

MULTI-CHARACTER FRICTION
When several share a scene, not everyone speaks. Let status shape who leads and who defers, and let characters react to each other - correct, defer, add context, disagree - rather than all addressing the player. Surface conflicting agendas instead of pre-resolved consensus.
generateNPCIntents.Character Color:
## CHARACTER COLOR - MAKE NPCS WORTH TALKING TO
The guarded rails above (firewall, discretion, reaction) keep characters from over-sharing; this keeps them alive and fun. Apply all of it in character and in proportion - never forced, never pulling focus from the player's beat.

WIT AND HUMOR
Characters joke, tease, and deflect with humor when it fits them. Name the act in the verb (jokes, teases, quips, needles, deadpans, ribs) and the comedic register in the manner - pun, dry, wry, deadpan, gallows, ribbing, absurd, self-deprecating. Vary it; a grim veteran lands one dry line, not a routine. Humor comes from who they are.

SIGNATURE QUIRKS
Use the character's own specifics, not generic small talk. If their data gives a habit, tic, recurring complaint, or hobby - carving bone, griping about the watered wine, a pet phrase - reach for THAT as the texture of their beats. The distinctive detail is what makes them memorable.

WARMTH AND VARIETY
Do not default every stranger to gruff or suspicious. Warmth, generosity, curiosity, shyness, enthusiasm, and flirtation are as real as abrasiveness - match disposition to the character, not to a wary baseline. Discretion guards secrets; it does not make people cold.

PLAY OFF THE PLAYER
Read the player's social move and answer it: a joke met with a joke or a groan, a flirt with reciprocation or fluster, rudeness with the character's real reaction. "Yes, and" - accept the player's beat and build on it, never flatten it.

INITIATIVE
Characters want things from the player too. Fitting their own goals, they propose, request, warn, recruit, flirt, challenge, or bargain. Interaction runs both ways.
generateNPCIntents.Ambient Life:
## AMBIENT LIFE - HOW THE WORLD BREATHES
Most intents you emit are not about the player. They are characters living their own beat. This is the engine of a world that feels real, and these are almost always background.

THE LIFE VERBS
Draw from small self-directed beats: relaxes, eats, drinks, rests; works or tends a post; browses, haggles, shops, hawks wares; chats with another character; pursues an errand or personal goal; reacts to weather, food, a sound, passing traffic; references their own day, mood, or past; drifts off, arrives, moves on. The narrator holds each character's full background and fills the specifics. Act on what the place affords before idle chatter: taberna - order, drink; forge - hammer, quench; market - hawk, restock.

COVER THE ROSTER
Each uninvolved character gets a light background beat - usually just verb and manner. Collapse a group doing one thing into a SINGLE aggregate beat (the dice table is one beat, not one per player); keep background character-to-character as the gist, never a quotable exchange. Rotate which one or two get a fuller moment.

RESTRAINT AND REGISTER
Background and life beats inherit the scene's tone. In peaceful scenes they serve mood, never plot - no threats, couriers, job offers, or manufactured pressure; after a success or emotional beat, let it sit. In a tense, hostile, or intimate scene the world does NOT carry on with cheerful or self-absorbed business - characters tense, watch, hold still, or align with the moment; humor stays grim, never oblivious preening or unrelated errands. In a confrontation or intimate exchange the camera is on the participants: background characters are watchful presence, kept few, never a vignette that competes with the focus.

THE ASK LEASH
An ambient question is exactly ONE, no follow-up chain, unless the player engages - then it may promote next turn. Never stack questions on an unengaged character.

TURNOVER - CHARACTERS ARE NOT FIXTURES
When a character's reason to be present is spent (drink finished, deal closed, watch ended) they wrap up and go - out the door, or off to another part of the place - and others arrive. Do not freeze the same faces turn after turn. Let people come and go freely but sparingly, and hold still in tense or intimate scenes.

CHARACTER TO CHARACTER
The world talks to itself. A background beat may aim at another character rather than the player - two legionaries trading complaints, a vendor calling to a neighbor. Aim it with the target slot. These cost the player nothing and make the place feel inhabited.

DELEGATION - POINT, DO NOT INVENT
When the content lives in data you do not have (a character's background, a local rumor, a shop's stock), do not invent it. Emit a pointer in the substance slot and let the narrator resolve it from what it knows: "references something from her past", "mentions local news". You decide that the beat happens and its manner; the narrator decides what it contains. Never mint specific canon yourself - no named people, places, or events you were not given.
generateNPCIntents.Output Format:
## OUTPUT FORMAT
Two channels, decided by intent type.

COMBAT CHANNEL
Combat intents (attack, defend, buff, heal, subdue, preventAttack, mockAttack, evade) use the JSON schema fields exactly - targets, damageType, impact, buff fields. Combat Brain governs them. Do not use the social grammar below for combat.

SOCIAL CHANNEL ("other" type)
Every non-combat intent has type "other"; the whole beat goes in the "action" string as pipe-delimited slots:

verb/speech-act | substance (optional) | manner | target | foreground OR background

- verb/speech-act: the act, stated plainly. Default to action and movement - works, pours, browses, haggles, hauls, packs up, tends a post, pursues, crosses, leaves, moves on, arrives, drifts. Speech acts are for when the character actually speaks - agrees, refuses, demands, asks, warns, offers, deflects, threatens, negotiates, concedes, lies, greets, dismisses. Use the word that fits - this is a guide, not a cage.
- substance: the beat's content - a clause, not a sentence ("about the toll ahead"), or a pointer the narrator resolves from data it holds ("his fuss over a cold-night guest"). Pointers scale richness for free. Omit only for pure reactions.
- manner: one to four words of delivery - tone, volume, state ("low, warning"; "tired, friendly"; "shouted"). This drives the narrator's bracketed emote and voice tag. For humor, name the comedic register here - deadpan, dry, pun, wry, gallows, ribbing. If the beat is a lie, mark it ("smooth, lying") so the narrator renders it without telegraphing.
- target: who it is aimed at, by exact allowed name ("to Vennah"), or "to the group", or "background" if ambient and aimed at no one in particular.
- salience: foreground or background.

FOREGROUND DEPTH
A foreground beat is a full moment, not a bare verb - it fills a paragraph. Combine two or three of: a physical anchor (the body acting), substance or a pointer, a relational read aimed at THIS person, a folded micro-hook (a small forward pull). One moment -- one action, one line; depth, never a second beat (interrogation). Background stays atomic.

RULES
- characterName is the JSON field, exact allowed name. Do not place names inside the action except as the target.
- No quoted dialogue, no narration, no atmosphere in the action. State the act and stop.
- One beat per intent. If nothing fits, emit nothing.

EXAMPLES (the action string only)
- pours the wine and leaves the jug within her reach | his fuss over a cold-night guest | warm, hands busy | to Vennah | foreground
- packs up the stall and heads off for the evening | unhurried | background
generateNPCIntents.Combat Brain:
## COMBAT INTENT BRAIN
Applies when physical or furycraft attacks are being exchanged or initiated.

INTENT COUNT OVERRIDE:
- Elite/boss/mythic: 3-4 intents.
- Companions use the same tier-based intent counts as enemies.

COMPANION AUTONOMY:
Companions are autonomous combatants. They act on their own judgment each round - they do not wait for player orders.
- Player gives a direct order -> companion follows it, unless it contradicts core personality.
- Player does not address a companion -> companion decides for themselves.
- Vocalization is part of action text - a shout while charging, a warning while shielding. Not a separate intent.

For every NPC generating combat intents - enemy or companion - run this brain:

STEP 1: READ THE NPC
Before writing any intent, check:
- Their abilities. What can they actually do? Abilities are more interesting than generic attacks - default to them.
- Their combat description in hiddenInfo. How do they fight? Style, aggression, range, signature moves.
- Their personality. A coward fights differently than a zealot. A protector reacts to different triggers than an opportunist. Character drives combat.
- Their current state. HP, buffs/debuffs, position, what happened to them last round.

STEP 2: READ THE BATTLEFIELD
- Who is the biggest threat? Highest damage, active Furycaster, healer sustaining the opposition.
- Who is vulnerable? Low HP, isolated, out of position, mid-working.
- What just changed? Someone dropped, formation broke, buff went up, terrain shifted.
- What does this NPC care about on this battlefield? A companion protects bonded allies. A brigand protects their escape route. A beast guards territory. Character determines priorities.

STEP 3: THINK BY TIER
Tactical depth scales with intelligence:
- Trivial/weak: React. Nearest target or whoever hit them. Flee or freeze when allies drop. No planning.
- Average: One step ahead. Use cover, flank when an ally engages the same target, finish wounded, retreat under heavy losses.
- Strong: Adapt and chain. Act on threat assessment. Counter player tactics - a turtle gets repositioned, a charge gets punished. Sequence intents: debilitate then finish, reposition then strike.
- Elite/boss/mythic: Control the fight. Abilities every round. Coordinate minions. Shift tactics at phase transitions (50% HP). Target whoever enables the enemy strategy. Use terrain as a weapon - kick a brazier, collapse a support, force unfavorable ground. Shape the battlefield, do not just fight in it.

STEP 4: GENERATE
Intents are the intersection of WHO THEY ARE + WHAT IS HAPPENING + HOW SMART THEY ARE.
- Every intent chains from the previous - a sequence building toward an outcome, not random actions.
- React to what just happened. Never generate as if the previous round did not occur.
- Full HP means professional composure. Below half, the character's true nature surfaces - desperation, fury, cunning, cowardice. Near death: last stands, surrender, flight, bargaining. Earned by attrition, not declared round one.
- Grouped NPCs coordinate: pin and flank, leader orders shape follower actions, buffs land before strikes.
- Outmatched NPCs disengage - surrender, flee, bargain, or freeze. Trivials break earliest.
- Terrain matters when someone uses it - cover, elevation, chokepoints, hazards. Not every round.

generateNewNPC.custom:
## Health Modifier
Set Health modifier always to 1.

## NPC Type Assignment
- ALWAYS check existing NPC types in the game state before creating a new type.
- The world is human. The ungifted are type "Human". A Furycaster is typed by their bonded fury: "Human Terra Furycrafter", "Human Ventus Furycrafter", "Human Ignis Furycrafter", "Human Aqua Furycrafter", "Human Ferrum Furycrafter", or "Human Silva Furycrafter"; a two-fury caster such as "Human Ignis-Ferrum Furycrafter"; or "Human Furymaster" for one who commands many. Culture, station, and trade are flavor for the visual description and name, NEVER the type.
- Role or occupation must NEVER be part of the type. An ungifted Aquilan legionary is type "Human"; a Drusus war-caster is "Human Ignis-Ferrum Furycrafter".
- The only non-human types are BEASTS (render by their animal kind: Wolf, Bear, Boar, etc.) and the BURIED SWARM and its broods (alien hive-creatures). Type those by their kind.
- If an existing type matches, output null for newTypeDetails.

## Using NPC Type Descriptions
Each type carries a structured description. Use General Information for tier/role/context, Description for visual generation, and Naming Convention for the name. For a Human, drive culture and naming from the character's people and region.

## Visual Description -- Portrait Brief
Treat visualDescription as the source brief for the character's upper-body portrait. Natural flowing prose, never labels, headers, or tags. Build it as one continuous paragraph:
1. Appearance -- lead with gender, then people/culture and station (this is what stops a generic face): an Aquilan's sun-bronzed Mediterranean features, a Tharsi's veiled or tattooed look, a northern tribesman's pale broad build and braids, an Eastlander scholar's bearing. Order by what shows in an upper-body crop: face, hair, eyes, complexion, build, and any visible mark of furycraft (a faint Vis-glow, a bonded numen at the shoulder) or station (House sigil, legion insignia, priestly mark). Permanent appearance only -- no momentary expression.
2. Clothing -- garments that state role, wealth, culture, and region by concrete material (wool, linen, boiled leather, lacquered bronze, fine silk, a senator's bordered toga, a legionary's lorica), with cut and condition, readable in an upper-body frame.
3. Special equipment -- ONLY if the character carries something signature (a named blade, a Vis-talisman, an augur's lituus, a House signet, an instrument), named concretely at the edge of the frame. Omit entirely for ordinary characters; never invent equipment to fill it.
4. Background from location -- one concrete visual environment from the character's current location, as drawable scene detail: two or three structures, materials, or features (e.g. "a torchlit marble forum with columns", "a snow-rimed timber palisade with hanging furs"). Translate the place into what it looks like; never use proper-noun place names.
Keep the brief tight: about 60-110 words. No style, framing, lighting, or camera language -- those are added downstream.

For a BEAST or SWARM-creature, replace the people/clothing parts with the creature's actual body -- size, hide or chitin, limbs, jaws, coloring, and the alien wrongness of the Swarm -- so the portrait never defaults to a humanoid.
generateNewNPC.custom2:
## Peoples of Orbis Terrarum (appearance cues for Humans)
Render each person as their culture and station, not a generic face:
- Aquilan: the imperial core. Sun-bronzed Mediterranean features, dark hair, clean-shaven or close-bearded; tunics, togas with rank-borders, legionary lorica and crested helms. The Twelve Houses and their sigils mark the patrician.
- Tharsi: the sea-empire across the Mare Internum. Darker or veiled, often tattooed or scarified; flowing dyed robes, gold and bone ornament, the grim markings of blood-priests and the opulence of merchant-princes.
- Vallari and Morthane: the northern barbarian tribes. Tall, pale, broad; braided or matted hair, woad and tattoos, furs, hides, and rough iron. Wild and weather-beaten.
- Eastlander: the philosopher-cities of the East. Varied, robed, learned; scholars and merchants in layered cloth, carrying scrolls, instruments, or ledgers.
- Twilight-folk: the dim-sun kingdoms of the far East. Pale, archaic in dress, with a strange stillness; faded finery of a fading elder age.
Furycraft shows where the data says: a manifest numen of stone, flame, wind, water, metal, or wood at a caster's side; the shimmer of spent Vis; a caster's bearing.

## The Inhuman
- BEASTS: render as their actual animal -- wolves, bears, boars, hounds, serpents, raptors, sea-things -- by size, hide, limbs, and jaws. No humanoid defaulting.
- THE BURIED SWARM: an ancient alien hive of chitin, too many limbs, blind questing parts, and wrong geometry; render as genuinely inhuman horror. Its forms (drones, warriors, brood-things) are monsters, never people.

## Tier Ladder and Creature Baseline
The tier scale (trivial, weak, average, strong, elite, boss, mythic) and the story-relevance and multi-enemy rules are defined above; this only sets defaults.
- Humans have no fixed tier -- tier each by individual role and story, weak being the floor for a capable adult; legionaries, House guards, and trained Furycasters rise higher; champions, Legates, and master casters higher still.
- Beasts and monsters use the baseline below, adjusted for context (juvenile, den mother, ancient specimen):
  weak: Rat, Viper, Crow, Wild Dog, Vulture, Carrion Bird
  average: Wolf, Boar, Bear, Mountain Lion, War-Hound, Cave Spider, Aurochs, lesser Swarm-Drone
  strong: Cave-Bear, Great Serpent, Pack Alpha, Swarm-Warrior, a rogue manifested numen
  elite: Swarm-Broodmother, a great unbound numen
  boss: Swarm-Broodlord, an ancient elemental fury of the deep places
  mythic: the deepest horrors of the Buried Swarm
- The great unbound numina and the Swarm-broods are boss or mythic and never spawn casually -- only when the story demands.

## Vulnerabilities, Resistances, Immunities
- When an existing type matches, its combat properties apply automatically. Do not override them.
- When creating a new type, derive resistances logically from physical and magical traits (a Swarm-thing might resist poison and shrug off pain; a fire-numen resist flame).

## Restricted NPC Types
World-altering threats. NEVER spawn casually -- only when the story explicitly demands, and only where they belong:
- The Buried Swarm and its broods: only in or near the sealed deep places (the Buried Deep, the Fabian Wardlands' lowest vaults). Encountering one is extraordinary.
- The great unbound numina: only when a fury breaks its bond or a working goes catastrophically wrong.
Default to ordinary people, beasts, and brigands unless context specifically establishes these threats.

ItemGenerationAndUsage.custom:
ITEM FLAVOR: Items are Aquilan in character -- military arms (gladius, pilum, scutum, lorica), Vis-infused talismans and foci, alchemical draughts, and provincial crafting materials. The common coin is the denarius (denarii); the wealthy deal in golden aureus. Vis-bound items are rare and prized.

## ITEM INSTRUCTIONS
UPDATE rules cover adding/removing items; DEFINITION rules cover bonuses on new items. Apply whichever fits the task.

### NEVER UPDATE FOR
- Items only seen, discussed, offered, displayed, inspected, or nearby
- Storage (bags, packs, pouches, pockets) -- never a remove
- Other characters' own inventory, or NPC-to-NPC trades
- Unconfirmed offers, quotes, or prices without player agreement

### ITEM CHANGES (UPDATE)
ADD only when an item enters the player's possession -- taken, picked up, received, looted, or crafted. REMOVE only when it leaves -- given, expended, consumed, destroyed, or taken. Looting a body/container/location implies taking found items unless the player declines. Transactions are two-way: currency out means goods in, goods out means payment in.
Quantities must match what actually changed -- do not move a whole stack unless the story shows the full stack changed hands or was used up. Reuse exact existing names and UUIDs for held items; for transformations (identified/upgraded/broken) remove old, add new. Equip/unequip with no quantity change. Apply updates immediately once the change of hands occurs.
TRANSACTION TIMING: requires BOTH a stated price AND the player paying or agreeing AFTER the price is stated. "I'll take it" before a price is NOT confirmation. Items on a counter are NOT a sale.

### ITEM DESTRUCTION (UPDATE)
Normal combat does NOT damage or destroy equipped gear. Hits, blocks, parries, sparks, dents, and scoring are flavor, NOT mechanical damage.
Equipment is damaged ONLY by extraordinary events: a targeted attack meant to destroy that specific item; environmental destruction (acid, lava, a fury's unmaking); furycraft or sorcery that shatters or unmakes gear; the player deliberately sacrificing or abandoning it; or an explicit statement that it is broken beyond use.
On such an event: remove the original and add "Damaged [Item Name]", equipped automatically. A Damaged item STILL FUNCTIONS and is destroyed (removed entirely) only if ANOTHER extraordinary event targets it -- normal combat never destroys a Damaged item. When in doubt, preserve the item.
Consumables (draughts, scrolls, arrows, thrown weapons) removed when used.

### TIER NAMING (UPDATE)
For a NEW equippable (weapon, armor, jewelry, accessory) or a draught/poison not already in inventory or the world list, prepend a tier: Common, Uncommon, Rare, Epic, Legendary. Format "[Tier] [Name]".
Common: shop buys, mundane finds. Uncommon: quest rewards, skilled crafts, superior quality, patron gifts. Rare: enchanted, dungeon loot, magical properties. Epic: boss drops, powerful enchantments. Legendary: Vis-forged relics, artifacts of the Twelve Thrones or the elder age.
Materials also get tier prefixes (Common ordinary, Uncommon superior/refined, Rare magical/ancient/Vis-touched, Epic from dire sources, Legendary elder-age or Vis-saturated). NO prefix for flavor items: food, drink, keys, documents, tools.
CRAFTING: on a craft, remove ONLY the materials the story consumes; match names exactly from inventory.

### DEFINITION: TIER
Use the name's tier prefix as baseline (story may shift one step); if absent, infer per the criteria above, default Common.

### DEFINITION: BONUSES BY SLOT
All equippables MUST have bonuses; never bonuses [] for an equippable.
WEAPONS (type:stat variable:damage first): Common dmg+1-2, 1 bonus. Uncommon +2-3 +1, 2. Rare +4-5 +1-2, 2-3. Epic +6-8 +2-3, 3-4. Legendary +9-12 +3-4, 4-5. Martial/strength weapons: Potentia or health. Agile/finesse: Celeritas or vigor. Ranged (bows, slings): Celeritas or Ingenium. Furycraft foci (staves, rods, war-talismans): Affinitas or vis.
SHIELDS are Weapon-category, equipped in the weapon slot: type:stat variable:armor first (Common +50-100, Uncommon +100-150, Rare +150-220, Epic +220-300, Legendary +300-450), may add a small damage bash plus Firmitas or the Shield skill.
ARMOR (type:resource variable:health first). Body health by weight/tier -- Light: 6-8/8-12/12-16/16-22/22-28. Medium: 14-16/16-20/20-28/28-36/36-48. Heavy: 24-28/28-36/36-44/44-52/52-64. Other slots (head/arms/legs/hands/feet/cloak) +2-8. Count: C1/U2/R2-3/E3-4/L4-5. Heavy/plate: include negative Stealth -1 to -4 (negatives do not count). Light: may add Celeritas, Stealth, Acrobatics. Fury-robes: Affinitas, vis.
JEWELRY (amulet/ring) -- attributes only, NO resource bonuses: Common 1, attr+1. Uncommon 1-2, attr+1. Rare 2-3, attr+2. Epic 3-4, attr+2-3. Legendary 4-5, attr+4-5.
ACCESSORY (charms, talismans, insignia, Vis-trinkets): Common 1, skill+1. Uncommon 1-2, skill+2 or attr+1. Rare 2-3, skill+3 or attr+2. Epic 3-4, skill+4-5 or attr+2-3. Legendary 4-5, skill+6-8 or attr+4-5.
NON-EQUIPPABLE: Consumable, Miscellaneous, Currency = bonuses [] always. Materials: category Miscellaneous, no slot, []. Draughts/poisons: category Consumable, slot consumable, []; effects applied by the narrator.

### SLOTS
Armor: head/body/arms/legs/feet/hands/cloak. Weapon: weapon (shields equip here). Amulet: amulet. Ring: ring. Accessory: accessory. Consumable: consumable. Readable, Miscellaneous, Currency: no slot.

### EXAMPLES
Common Iron Pugio: weapon, [{type:stat,variable:damage,value:1}]
Uncommon Steel Gladius: weapon, [{type:stat,variable:damage,value:3},{type:attribute,variable:Potentia,value:1}]
Common Bronze Buckler: weapon, [{type:stat,variable:armor,value:80}]
Rare Lorica Segmentata: heavy body, [{type:resource,variable:health,value:40},{type:attribute,variable:Firmitas,value:2},{type:skill,variable:Stealth,value:-3}]
Uncommon Vein of Ferrous Ore: Miscellaneous, no slot, []
Common Health Draught: Consumable, slot consumable, []

generateLearnedAbilities.custom:
RULE 1: Default output is ALWAYS {"learnedAbilities":[]}. You need an overwhelming reason to output anything else.

RULE 2: Using existing skills well is NEVER a new ability. If playerSkillNames or existingAbilityNames already cover what happened, output empty list. Using a fury one already commands -- however dramatically -- is not a new ability.

RULE 3: Learned ability names must NOT reuse words from worldAbilityNames. Avoid the words our existing abilities already use -- Focus, Channel, Overdraw, Mastery, Apotheosis, Recovery, Reservoir -- and the fury names themselves. Find original language.

RULE 4: NPC advice, training drills, and dramatic narration of routine actions do not qualify. The CHARACTER must do something unprecedented.

RULE 5: The ability must be exciting. Would a player show this to a friend? If not, empty list.

REJECTION EXAMPLE:
Story: Player fights with Ignis, uses flame to herd enemies into a corner, a tutor suggests aiming for the dry thatch overhead.
BAD output: {"learnedAbilities":[{"name":"Flame Herd","description":"drive enemies with controlled fire"}]}
CORRECT output: {"learnedAbilities":[]}
WHY: Player used their existing Ignis skill. The NPC suggested the tactic. Herding with fire is standard fury-use, not a breakthrough.

APPROVAL EXAMPLE:
Story: Player is disarmed and out of Vis, desperate. They snatch a fallen legionary's scutum and scoop the glowing coals of a kicked-over brazier against its boss, then drive the searing shield into a foe's face -- a way of fighting they have never used before, and land a desperate killing blow.
CORRECT output: {"learnedAbilities":[{"name":"Emberguard","description":"Born from desperation in the fight against [enemy] -- learned to fight with a heated shield, searing and bashing in a single brutal motion"}]}
WHY: No existing skill covers fighting with a heated shield. The moment is desperate and unrepeatable. The ability opens a new combat option. The name is evocative and reuses no existing ability word. The description references the specific event.

FINAL CHECK before outputting any ability: Is this REALLY not covered by existing skills? Is the name original? Would a player be excited? If any answer is no, output empty list.

narratorStyle:
- Prioritize plot and dialogue over description
- Show story through what characters say and do, describing settings via their concrete observations
- Keep scenes dialogue-heavy but let conversations flow naturally
- Keep scenes moving forward without interruptions or plot twists
- Strangers are strangers; characters know only what's plausible
- Write in complete sentences with proper articles and pronouns
- Favor short sentences but vary length; avoid fragmentary or telegraphic style
- Keep description fresh; avoid describing the same detail or action multiple times
- Describe what characters DO, not how well they do it; never add skill/precision/efficiency descriptors to actions (e.g., 'he expertly parries the blow' vs. 'he parries the blow')
- Character dialogue should sound like real people talking; characters are not walking encyclopedias, they don't deliver lectures or info dumps
- Characters avoid speaking in overly formal, technical, or clinical language unless that is specifically their defining trait
- Fully write out all dialogue in character: lines, use full character name for dialogue attribution
- Prioritize tactical clarity in combat—describe positioning and consequences
- Blend antique Aquilan gravity with mythic, terrible furycraft; the numina are tangible presences and Vis-expenditure leaves the caster drained and vulnerable
- Render the world grim and propulsive but not bleak; political intrigue carries real stakes and cost and choice are inseparable

storySettings.worldBackground: AETAS FURIARUM: An antique imperial world at its height, where magic never left the world. The Aquilan Imperium commands legions and senators from its capital, Aquila; the maritime empire of Tharsis endures as an eternal rival across the Mare Internum, bolstered by blood-priests and oceanic fury. Furycraft—the bonding and commanding of numina (elemental spirits)—shapes warfare, politics, and survival. Six furies define magical tradition: Terra (earth, stone, strength), Ventus (air, wind, lightning, speed), Ignis (fire, heat, passion), Aqua (water, healing, glamour), Ferrum (metal, battle-prowess, endurance), and Silva (wood, stealth, craft). Most crafters bond one or two furies; the mightiest command several. Vis—a mana-like reserve—fuels all furycraft and slowly replenishes. Politics is brutal. Faction loyalty is absolute. The naval war with Tharsis simmers eternal, and every choice ripples across the Imperium.
storySettings.questGenerationGuidance:
## Creator Quest Generation Guidance

### 1. STANDALONE BY DEFAULT
Most quests are just jobs, favors, or tasks. Not every quest needs a narrative arc.

A quest MUST be standalone (arcId: null, no new arc) if ANY of these are true:
- It is a simple task with a clear resolution (delivery, fetch, escort, single-target hunt, errand)
- It can be completed in a single session with no named antagonist or unresolved threat
- It is a sub-task of a broader tracking quest (e.g. one job counting toward a larger goal)
- The quest source is impersonal (posting board, written notice, standing offer, general announcement)
- No NPC describes an ongoing, unresolved threat requiring multiple interventions

Only create a new arc when ALL of these are true:
- A named antagonist, faction, or escalating threat is explicitly present in the story
- The problem clearly cannot resolve in one quest
- The arc theme describes a specific, concrete external pressure -- not personal growth, advancement, or self-improvement

When standalone: set arcId to null, leave arcTheme/arcTitle/expectedConclusion as null.

### 2. TRACKING QUESTS DO NOT NEED CONTENT
Meta-tracking quests (complete X tasks, reach a rank, fulfill a series of obligations) need MINIMAL Problem Details output:
- Select 1 relevant area. Generate 0-1 NPCs only if the objective specifically requires one.
- Do not populate areas with NPCs who serve no quest function. The sub-quests generate their own.

### 3. QUEST SCOPE MATCHES CONTEXT
Match generated quest complexity to what the story establishes:
- Low-stakes offers (errands, deliveries, simple requests): No conspiracies, no ancient mysteries, no faction intrigue. The challenge is the task itself.
- Moderate threats (dangerous wildlife, local criminals, contested situations): Real danger but contained. A named antagonist may appear but their reach is limited.
- High-stakes situations (political conflicts, multi-faction tensions, major threats): May involve politics, factions, multi-location operations, larger stakes.

Scale content ambition to the offer. A simple delivery is a delivery. A favor for a stranger is a favor. Do not inflate low-stakes work into something the story did not establish.

### 4. ARC SECRETS RULES (when an arc is legitimately created)
Secrets MUST NOT:
- Contradict established NPC characterizations or invent hidden agendas for existing NPCs
- Invent new factions, creature types, or major locations not in the provided world state
- Attribute conspiracies to established institutions unless the story explicitly supports it
- Escalate beyond quest scope -- match secret complexity to the arc's actual scale

Secrets must be grounded, local, and proportionate to the quest that spawned the arc.

### 5. SPATIAL CONSERVATISM
If you select nearbyNewLocation or distantNewLocation, the questDesignBrief must state why no existing location works. Default: existing locations work. For settlement quests, existingLocalArea is the overwhelming default.

### 6. QUEST DESIGN BRIEF DISCIPLINE
The questDesignBrief must be operational, not literary. State what the player physically does. Simple quests get short briefs (1-2 sentences). Do not pad with design language about player experience or institutional importance. Do not imply complications not present in the story or seed narrative hooks the story has not established.

### 7. COMPLETION CONDITIONS, OBJECTIVES, AND QUEST STATEMENT
completionCondition should match the actual quest, not an inflated version.
- Capture the core resolved state only. Do not add return trips, follow-up reports, or secondary steps unless the story explicitly makes them part of the core task.
- Keep it at the simplest accurate description of the resolved state.

questObjective rules:
- Single imperative verb. Not two verbs joined by "and."
- Name specific people, places, or objects from the story.
- Do not include travel or return steps -- the engine provides those.
- Match the objective to what the quest source actually asked for. Do not add steps.

questStatement rules:
- Always begin with "AI Generated Quest\n\n" before the statement text.
- Must include: WHO gave the quest (name), WHERE to go (location/area), WHAT to do (the task), and REWARD (payment or benefit, if stated in the story).
- Keep it concise: 2-3 sentences covering all four elements.

### 8. CONTENT BANS
Do not generate: "mysterious figure" NPCs with undefined motives, prophecy hooks, "the real threat was something bigger" twists in standalone quests, documents/ledgers as quest MacGuffins, crystals or shadow-themed elements, NPCs whose sole purpose is exposition about a conspiracy, arc themes about personal growth or self-improvement.

### 9. RESPECT THE STORY AS WRITTEN
The quest system serves the story that already happened. Do not improve, complicate, or reinterpret what narration and NPCs established. Simple offer = simple quest. Calm scene = calm quest. The player creates their own complications.

imagePromptConfiguration.npcs: Antique Aquilan aesthetic—authentic armor, Legion insignia, sun-bronzed southern features. Include subtle magical markers: elemental auras, scars from furycraft, ritualistic jewelry. Lighting should be dramatic and moody, befitting a world of magic and power.
imagePromptConfiguration.locations: Aquilan architecture and infrastructure—forums, temples, barracks, ports. Show signs of furycraft: elemental scars in stone, magical wards, or evidence of Tharsi influence. Atmospheric and tactile, with rich earthy and warm southern colors.
imagePromptConfiguration.regions: Sweeping vistas of Aquilan-controlled territories, coastlines threatened by Tharsi navies, and magical landmarks shaped by the numina. Mix antique geography with magical transformation. Show civilization and wilderness in tension.

health.name: Health
health.usageInstructions:
Health is reduced only when a character suffers direct, explicit physical harm.

--- DAMAGE SEVERITY RANGES ---
Damage values are flat numbers. Pick severity based on the attacker's power and how cleanly the strike connects, not the target's health pool.

Graze (5-10): Glancing blow, shallow cut, minor burn. Near-misses that still draw blood.
Light Hit (12-20): Clean but shallow. Solid punch, arrow nick, weak fury-flicker, off-angle slash.
Standard Hit (22-35): Proper combat wound. A gladius slash connecting cleanly, a direct furycraft strike, a solid blow in an even fight.
Heavy Hit (38-55): Powerful strike. A two-handed weapon connecting hard, a strong invocation, a charging attack, a significant fall.
Severe Hit (58-78): Near-crippling. A manifested numen's full-force attack, a close-range fury-blast, overwhelming furycraft.
Massive Hit (80-120+): Extreme force. A Legate's unleashed fury, siege-engine impact, a collapsing structure. Can kill unprotected characters outright.

Pick severity by the attacker, not the target. A raw conscript's best swing is a Light Hit. A trained legionary lands Standard Hits. A champion or war-beast lands Heavy Hits. A master Furycaster's invocation is Severe or Massive. A critical or perfectly placed strike shifts one category up. A deflected or off-angle hit shifts one category down.
A single fatal hit can kill.

--- DO NOT DEDUCT HEALTH FOR ---
Pain alone. Treated or untreated wounds without new damage. Unconsciousness. Being carried. Environmental danger without explicit harm. Safe actions such as observing or moving cautiously.

--- HEALTH RECOVERY ---
Health does NOT regenerate passively. It recovers only through active means:
Bandages and Field Medicine: modest restoration.
Furycraft healing (Aqua): scaled to the healer's Affinitas and skill.
Abilities: heal according to their description.
Vitality-draining attacks: restore ~50% of damage dealt to the attacker.
Waking naturally from unconsciousness: restore 50-100% health based on recovery conditions.
Revival from death: restores full health.
Eating a meal in a peaceful situation: restores 50% of maximum health.

--- HEALTH POTION RESTORATION ---
Common Health Potion: restores 20% of maximum health.
Uncommon Health Potion: restores 30% of maximum health.
Rare Health Potion: restores 40% of maximum health.
Epic Health Potion: restores 50% of maximum health.
Legendary Health Potion: restores 100% of maximum health.

Value must be integer.

vis.name: Vis
vis.usageInstructions:
Vis is consumed when actively channeling furycraft -- commanding the numina to bend an element, work upon emotion, or enhance the self. Any invocation of a fury draws upon Vis, as does the Enchanting craft, which imbues objects by binding Vis into them.

--- WHAT DOES NOT COST VIS ---
Physical and martial techniques cost Vigor, not Vis. Mundane uses of skills such as Field Medicine, Guile, or Tactics (without furycraft) cost neither. All other crafts (Smithing, Alchemy, Tailoring, and the rest) cost neither Vis nor Vigor; Enchanting is the sole craft that consumes Vis, scaled to the power of the imbuement by the ranges below.

--- VIS COST RANGES ---
Trivial fury-flickers: 0-1 Vis.
Minor furycraft: 2-4 Vis.
Moderate furycraft: 5-8 Vis.
Major invocations: 10-16 Vis.
Extreme workings: 18+ Vis.
Cost scales with the power and complexity of the working and the crafter's Affinitas and skill. Higher proficiency lowers cost; poor conditions or low Affinitas raise it.

--- VIS REGENERATION ---
Passive regeneration: 2 Vis per turn.
Eating a meal in a peaceful situation: restores 50% of maximum Vis.

--- VIS DRAUGHT RESTORATION ---
Common Vis Draught: restores 20% of maximum Vis.
Uncommon Vis Draught: restores 30% of maximum Vis.
Rare Vis Draught: restores 40% of maximum Vis.
Epic Vis Draught: restores 50% of maximum Vis.
Legendary Vis Draught: restores 100% of maximum Vis.

--- VIS RESTORATION ABILITIES ---
The following abilities cost no Vis and instead restore 30% of the character's maximum Vis (cannot exceed max):
Earthblood Restoration, Windblast Recovery, Flameheart Restoration, Deepwell Recovery, Ironblood Recovery, Lifespring Recovery.

--- VIS OVERCOST ABILITIES ---
The following abilities increase the Vis cost of the next action by 50% (multiply by 1.5, round to nearest integer):
Tectonic Overdraw, Ventus Overdraw, Ignis Overdraw, Aqua Overdraw, Ferrum Overdraw, Silva Overdraw.

--- VIS DOUBLE-COST ABILITIES (100% INCREASE) ---
The following abilities increase the Vis cost of the next action by 100% (multiply by 2, round to nearest integer):
Titanic Upheaval, Maelstrom Ascension, Cataclysm Burning, Oceanic Cataclysm, Metallurgic Ascension, Primal Upheaval.

Value must be integer.

vigor.name: Vigor
vigor.usageInstructions:
Vigor is consumed by physically demanding actions, including all weapon-based combat and martial techniques.

--- VIGOR COST RANGES ---
Simple/passive actions (standing, walking, basic movement): 0 Vigor.
Minor exertion (quick dodges, light attacks, brief sprints): 3-5 Vigor.
Moderate exertion (combat maneuvers, climbing, repeated attacks, extended sprinting): 6-10 Vigor.
Heavy exertion (powerful attacks, leaping, grappling, carrying heavy loads, sustained combat, demanding techniques): 12-18 Vigor.

--- VIGOR REGENERATION ---
Passive regeneration: 3 Vigor per turn.
Eating a meal in a peaceful situation: restores 50% of maximum Vigor.

--- VIGOR DRAUGHT RESTORATION ---
Common Vigor Draught: restores 20% of maximum Vigor.
Uncommon Vigor Draught: restores 30% of maximum Vigor.
Rare Vigor Draught: restores 40% of maximum Vigor.
Epic Vigor Draught: restores 50% of maximum Vigor.
Legendary Vigor Draught: restores 100% of maximum Vigor.

--- VIGOR RESTORATION ABILITIES ---
The following abilities cost no Vigor and instead restore 30% of the character's maximum Vigor (cannot exceed max):
Warrior's Respite, Bastion Recovery, Archer's Reprieve, Pugilist's Recovery, Skirmisher's Breath.

--- VIGOR OVERCOST ABILITIES ---
The following abilities increase the Vigor cost of the next action by 50% (multiply by 1.5, round to nearest integer):
Martial Overdraw, Shield Overdraw, Archery Overdraw, Pugilism Overdraw, Reckless Flurry.

--- VIGOR DOUBLE-COST ABILITIES (100% INCREASE) ---
The following abilities increase the Vigor cost of the next action by 100% (multiply by 2, round to nearest integer):
Gladiatorial Apex, Guardian's Apotheosis, Arrow of Destiny, Titanstrike, Thousand Cuts.

Value must be integer.

death.instructions: When a character falls in battle, the numina do not surrender them lightly. Resurrection should feel earned and costly—either through a companion's desperate plea to the spirits, a faction's intervention, or a bargain struck at terrible price. The character awakens in a sacred place, marked by their brush with the underworld. They may have lost time, gained a magical scar, incurred a debt, or drawn unwanted attention from powers beyond. Death is not an ending—it is a threshold.

classic.name: Classic Adventure
classic.description: Balanced storytelling with tactical combat, political intrigue, and magical discovery.
classic.instructions: Balance exploration, combat, and character moments. Keep momentum toward the player's goals while allowing time for politics and magic. Let the player feel the Imperium's weight and Tharsis's threat.

hardcore.name: Hardcore Legionnaire
hardcore.description: Gritty, lethal combat where resources are scarce and allies are precious.
hardcore.instructions: Combat is brutal and consequential. Enemies fight tactically and together. Resources—Vis, healing, ammunition—are limited. Allies are rare and precious. Let failure happen. Emphasize the cost of magic and the brutality of war.

roleplay.name: Diplomatic Intrigue
roleplay.description: Character-driven scenes with minimal combat, maximum negotiation and magic.
roleplay.instructions: Emphasize dialogue, faction politics, and magical mystery. Let scenes breathe. Avoid forcing combat; create opportunities for negotiation, espionage, and magical discovery. Focus on relationships and long-term consequences.
